E. The evidence supports that flame structure governs CO/CO2 ratio within this experiment, but does not establish whether this relationship holds when battery chemistry or venting gas composition changes substantially.
F. The higher CO/CO2 ratio in the second stage proves that the second stage generates more total CO mass than the first stage across all SOC conditions.

solver_view.stem: In a battery management system study, early-stage internal short circuit (ISC) in one cell within a multi-cell module produces a voltage difference of less than 0.1% between the faulty submodule and any normal submodule. Shannon entropy analysis of the same voltage signals shows approximately 10% relative difference between the faulty and normal submodules. The faulty cell's SOC is 2–10% lower than normal cells. A BMS engineer must select a detection strategy. Which conclusions are admissible?
options: A. Because the voltage difference is below 0.1%, threshold-based voltage monitoring cannot reliably detect early-stage ISC under these conditions.
B. Shannon entropy amplifies the ISC signal relative to direct voltage comparison, making it more suitable for early-stage ISC detection than threshold voltage methods under these conditions.
C. The 2–10% SOC deficit in the faulty cell is large enough to be directly detected by conventional voltage threshold methods, making entropy analysis redundant.
D. The Shannon entropy difference of approximately 10% confirms that the faulty cell has undergone complete thermal runaway, since only TR would produce such a large entropy signal.
E. The SOC reduction in the faulty cell contributes to increased inconsistency within the module, which is what the entropy-based signal is capturing rather than a direct ISC signature.
F. Threshold voltage methods remain valid for early-stage ISC detection provided the sampling frequency is increased, because the voltage signal is present but temporally aliased.
G. The diagnostic advantage of Shannon entropy over direct voltage difference is consistent with entropy being sensitive to distributional changes in signal patterns rather than mean-level shifts.

solver_view.stem: In a battery module, thermal runaway propagation (TRP) is being analyzed. Testing has established that approximately 12% of the total heat released by an initiating cell is sufficient to trigger thermal runaway in an adjacent cell, and that the majority of this inter-cell heat transfer occurs through the battery shell rather than through the pole connector. The module contains cells at different states of charge: some at 30% SOC and others at 85% SOC. An engineer must assess which statements about propagation risk and heat transfer pathway are supported by the available evidence. Which of the following conclusions are admissible?
options: A. Cells at 85% SOC will contribute to faster and more intense propagation than cells at 30% SOC if they enter thermal runaway, because higher SOC is associated with greater propagation intensity.

B. Because only 12% of the initiating cell's heat is required to trigger adjacent cell runaway, a module design that reduces shell-to-shell thermal conductance is more directly relevant to propagation prevention than one that focuses on pole connector insulation.
C. The 12% heat transfer threshold implies that if the initiating cell releases less than 12% of its total heat to neighbors, propagation cannot occur under any conditions.
D. Cells at 30% SOC cannot enter thermal runaway under any circumstances because lower SOC is associated with less intense propagation.

E. The dominance of shell-based heat transfer over pole-connector-based transfer means that thermal isolation strategies targeting the shell surface are more mechanistically justified than those targeting only the electrical connectors.
F. The 12% threshold for triggering adjacent cell runaway means that even modest reductions in inter-cell heat transfer through the shell could meaningfully affect whether propagation occurs.
G. Because the majority of heat transfers through the shell, increasing the electrical resistance of pole connectors will fully prevent thermal runaway propagation in this module.

solver_view.stem: In a confined battery enclosure, three cell configurations are tested: closely nested (no gap), 2 mm air gap, and 2 mm FR-PP thermal barrier. The FR-PP material melts between 148–160°C, begins decomposing at 280°C, and forms an insulating char up to 500°C. The air gap configuration shows radiation-dominated heat transfer with convective contributions only during venting. Which of the following conclusions are supported?
options: A. The FR-PP barrier prevents propagation partly because its phase-change absorption and subsequent char formation provide two sequential thermal resistance mechanisms rather than a single one.
B. In the air gap configuration, eliminating the venting/breaching phase would not significantly alter the dominant heat transfer mode, since radiation governs throughout the process.

C. The closely nested configuration produces higher peak enclosure pressure and temperature than the air gap configuration, indicating that direct cell contact accelerates both thermal and mechanical energy release.
D. The FR-PP barrier's effectiveness is primarily attributable to its convective blocking capability during venting, since radiation is already the dominant mode in the air gap scenario.
E. Because radiation dominates in the air gap scenario, replacing the air gap with a radiation-absorbing solid material would necessarily outperform the FR-PP barrier in all propagation metrics.
F. The 2 mm air gap delays propagation relative to closely nested cells because it eliminates conductive heat transfer between cells in direct contact.
G. The FR-PP barrier can be considered effective only if enclosure peak temperatures remain below its decomposition onset at 280°C, since char formation is not a designed protective function.

solver_view.stem: In a cylindrical cell pack, thermal contact resistance θc between a triggered cell and its neighbor is measured at two values: 0.5 K/W and 1.5 K/W. A threshold exists at 0.89 K/W separating two distinct propagation modes. At θc = 0.5 K/W, surface temperature exceeds the separator melting point before jellyroll average temperature does. At θc = 1.5 K/W, surface and jellyroll temperatures converge before reaching the melting threshold. Battery spacing is held constant. Which statements are admissible?
options: A. At θc = 0.5 K/W, thermal runaway is triggered earlier than at θc = 1.5 K/W because localized surface overheating produces an internal short circuit that accelerates the thermal runaway sequence.
B. At θc = 1.5 K/W, thermal runaway is eventually triggered when temperatures converge at the separator melting point, so higher contact resistance prevents thermal runaway initiation entirely.

C. Total propagation time is longer at θc = 1.5 K/W than at θc = 0.5 K/W, which is counterintuitive because higher contact resistance reduces direct heat flux yet results in a more extended propagation event.
D. The threshold at 0.89 K/W represents a regime boundary where the dominant triggering mechanism transitions from surface-localized short circuit to bulk jellyroll heating.
E. Reducing battery spacing at θc = 0.5 K/W would further accelerate propagation because both lower contact resistance and reduced spacing independently decrease equivalent thermal resistance.
F. At θc = 1.5 K/W the absence of early ISC means the cell is permanently protected from thermal runaway under all ambient conditions.

solver_view.stem: In a nail penetration test on a large-format lithium-ion battery pack, engineers measure a temperature difference of nearly 792°C within a single cell. They also find that heat conducted through electrode connectors is approximately one-tenth of that conducted through the cell shell. A phase change composite (PCC) material placed between cells reduces the maximum temperature of surrounding cells by 60°C. An engineer proposes that surface temperature monitoring of the shell provides a reliable real-time indicator of internal peak temperature during nail penetration events. Which statements correctly evaluate this proposal and the thermal management implications?
options: A. The ~792°C intra-cell temperature difference demonstrates that surface temperature measurements cannot reliably represent internal peak temperatures during nail penetration, directly invalidating the proposal.

B. Because heat transfer through the shell dominates over electrode connectors by a factor of ten, surface temperature sensors on the shell will overestimate internal peak temperature, since the shell conducts heat away faster than the interior generates it.

C. The 60°C reduction in neighboring cell temperature from PCC material addresses thermal propagation between cells but does not resolve the intra-cell temperature non-uniformity that makes surface monitoring unreliable.

D. The dominance of shell-based heat conduction over connector-based conduction implies that thermal management strategies targeting the shell surface will be more effective at controlling inter-cell propagation than strategies targeting electrode terminals.

E. Since PCC reduces neighboring cell temperature by 60°C, it can be concluded that PCC completely prevents thermal runaway propagation in any module configuration where cells are separated by PCC layers.

F. The large intra-cell temperature gradient during nail penetration indicates that electrochemical reactions are spatially uniform within the cell, since a uniform reaction would produce the observed temperature distribution.

solver_view.stem: Three cell groups—fresh cells, cells cycled at 25°C, and cells cycled at -5°C—all reach approximately 80% state of health. ARC testing reveals self-heating onset at ~102°C for fresh cells and ~82°C for low-temperature-cycled cells. Cells from a dynamically cycled consumer device show voltage drop at the lowest temperatures of all groups. An engineer proposes that SOH alone is sufficient to rank these cells by thermal runaway risk for field safety assessment. Which conclusions are admissible?
options: A. The 20°C reduction in self-heating onset for low-temperature-cycled cells is mechanistically linked to decreased activation energy, not to residual capacity difference.
B. SOH equivalence at 80% across groups is sufficient to conclude equivalent thermal runaway risk, because capacity is the primary determinant of heat release.
C. Cells with identical SOH but different aging histories can exhibit meaningfully different self-heating onset temperatures, making SOH an insufficient thermal risk classifier.
D. The consumer device cells' earlier voltage drop indicates greater sensitivity to internal short circuit triggers, which is not captured by their SOH value.
E. Because low-temperature cycling reduces activation energy, these cells will always exhibit lower peak thermal runaway temperatures than fresh cells regardless of test conditions.
F. Fresh cells exhibiting higher mass loss during thermal runaway than aged cells is consistent with greater available active material, not with higher SOH-normalized risk.

solver_view.stem: Two 18650 LiCoO2 batteries are tested under adiabatic conditions. One is charged to 4.2 V and the other is at 3.7 V (uncharged). The charged battery exhibits a sharp exponential increase in thermal reactive hazards beginning near 200°C, while the uncharged battery does not show this behavior. The porous separator in these cells is known to break down above 120°C, inducing internal short circuit. A safety engineer must assess which conclusions about the thermal hazard mechanism and the role of charge state are supported by these conditions. Which of the following are admissible?
options: A. The separator breakdown above 120°C is a necessary condition for thermal runaway initiation in both charged and uncharged batteries, since it induces internal short circuit in both cases.

B. The sharp exponential increase in thermal reactive hazards near 200°C in the charged battery is consistent with reactions that are strongly dependent on the electrochemical energy stored at high charge state, not merely on temperature alone.

C. Because the uncharged battery does not exhibit a sharp thermal hazard increase, it can be concluded that separator breakdown does not occur in uncharged batteries under the same heating conditions.

D. The difference in thermal reactive hazard behavior between charged and uncharged batteries indicates that charge state modulates the severity of exothermic reactions triggered after separator breakdown, not merely the onset temperature.

E. The heat of reaction being substantially higher for charged batteries than for uncharged batteries is consistent with additional energy release pathways that are only active when the battery is at elevated charge state.
F. The exponential hazard increase near 200°C in charged batteries proves that the separator breakdown at 120°C is the rate-limiting step controlling the overall thermal runaway energy release.

solver_view.stem: Two LFP cell formats—a larger-capacity cell (LFP-L) and a smaller-capacity cell (LFP-SS)—are tested under thermal runaway conditions. At 100% SOC, LFP-SS produces more gas per unit capacity than LFP-L across all test environments, yet LFP-L reaches a higher maximum temperature. At 110% SOC, the upper explosive limit (UEL) of LFP-SS gas becomes lower than that of LFP-L, attributed to further decomposition of higher hydrocarbons into low-carbon species. An engineer must select which statements about these two formats are admissible.
options: A. At 100% SOC, LFP-SS presents a wider flammable concentration range than LFP-L because both its LEL and UEL are higher, which directly implies greater ignition probability in a vented enclosure.

B. The higher maximum temperature of LFP-L at both 100% and 110% SOC, combined with lower gas production per unit capacity, suggests that LFP-L stores more energy in forms that manifest as heat rather than gas-phase products.

C. At 110% SOC, LFP-L gas has a higher UEL than LFP-SS, which is counterintuitive given LFP-SS produces more gas per capacity, and is explained by the composition shift toward low-carbon hydrocarbons in LFP-SS at elevated SOC.
D. Because LFP-SS produces more gas per capacity than LFP-L under all tested conditions, LFP-SS will always generate a larger absolute gas volume in any real installation regardless of cell capacity.

E. The presence of oxygen in an air environment increases peak pressure change for LFP-SS compared to nitrogen, indicating that enclosure atmosphere is a relevant variable in pressure hazard assessment for this cell format.
F. The reversal of UEL ordering between 100% and 110% SOC demonstrates that flammability limit comparisons between cell formats are SOC-dependent and cannot be generalized from a single charge level.

solver_view.stem: Two battery chemistries—sodium-ion (SIB) and lithium-ion (LIB)—are compared under two distinct operating regimes. Under normal discharge at 3C rate, SIBs generate less surface heat than LIBs by approximately 2.7°C. However, under 15% overcharge, SIBs generate more surface heat than LIBs by approximately 1.5°C and additionally exhibit full thermal runaway reaching a peak temperature near 274°C, while LIBs do not exhibit thermal runaway under the same overcharge heating conditions. At 15% overcharge, SIB residual capacity drops to 67.72% while LIB residual capacity remains at 99.07%. At 0°C and 0.5C discharge, SIBs retain 13.93% more capacity than LIBs. An engineer is evaluating these two chemistries for deployment in a stationary energy storage application that requires both low-temperature performance and tolerance of occasional overcharge events. Which of the following conclusions are admissible given the described evidence?
options: A. SIBs are preferable to LIBs for low-temperature discharge performance at moderate rates, but this advantage does not imply equivalent or superior safety under overcharge conditions.
B. Because SIBs generate less surface heat than LIBs under normal high-rate discharge, SIBs present a lower thermal hazard than LIBs across all operating conditions including abusive ones.

C. The thermal behavior of SIBs under overcharge represents a regime change relative to normal operation: the chemistry that is thermally cooler under normal discharge becomes the more thermally hazardous one under overcharge abuse.

D. The 33.35% gap in residual capacity between SIBs and LIBs at 15% overcharge is sufficient to conclude that SIBs have fundamentally inferior electrode materials compared to LIBs, regardless of operating condition.

E. For the described stationary application, selecting SIBs based solely on their low-temperature capacity advantage would be an incomplete engineering judgment because the overcharge safety behavior represents a qualitatively different failure mode.

F. The surface temperature difference of 1.5°C between SIBs and LIBs under 15% overcharge is the primary indicator that SIBs will undergo thermal runaway while LIBs will not, meaning surface temperature monitoring alone is sufficient to predict thermal runaway onset in this scenario.

solver_view.stem: Two black mass samples (A and C) that demonstrated smoldering fire hazard in self-heating ignition tests are subsequently tested for dust explosion risk. Neither sample produces a corrected pressure rise of 0.3 bar or more in a 20 L spherical chamber using 2 kJ pyrotechnic igniters across a wide range of dust concentrations. Neither can be ignited in a modified Hartmann apparatus at 1–10 J ignition energy. A process engineer proposes that because these samples are confirmed smoldering hazards, they should also be treated as dust explosion hazards in facility design. Which assessment is most defensible?
options: A. The proposal is not supported, because the explosion screening tests showed no pressure rise meeting the explosion threshold despite high ignition energy, and smoldering hazard does not imply dust explosion hazard.
B. The proposal is defensible, because any material capable of smoldering at temperatures above 600°C necessarily has sufficient energy density to support a dust explosion under confinement.

C. The screening test results are inconclusive regarding explosion hazard, because 2 kJ ignition energy may be insufficient to initiate explosion in some dust types regardless of their intrinsic reactivity.

D. The absence of a pressure rise in the 20 L chamber test under the stated conditions provides direct evidence that these samples do not meet the dust explosion classification threshold under those test conditions.
E. The smoldering behavior confirms that the samples contain sufficient reactive material to pose dust explosion risk if particle size is reduced below the tested range.
F. Dust explosion hazard and smoldering fire hazard are governed by different physical mechanisms, so a positive result in one test does not predict the outcome of the other.

solver_view.stem: Two cylindrical lithium-ion cell chemistries, NCA and NMC811, are subjected to identical accelerating rate calorimetry tests in a nitrogen atmosphere at 100% SOC. NCA cells reach thermal runaway onset at a slightly lower average temperature than NMC811 cells. However, NMC811 cells reach a higher average maximum temperature during thermal runaway, release a higher fraction of CO2 relative to CO, and produce approximately 66% more hydrocarbons. Additionally, prior to thermal runaway, NMC811 cells show a progressive increase in CO and CO2 release, whereas NCA cells show no such progressive pre-runaway gas increase and instead exhibit more abrupt gas ejection once thermal runaway begins. A battery safety engineer must assess which chemistry presents a greater fire and toxicity hazard and which offers better pre-runaway diagnostic opportunity. Which of the following conclusions are admissible?
options: A. NCA cells present a greater hazard than NMC811 cells because their lower onset temperature means thermal runaway is triggered under less extreme conditions.
B. The progressive pre-runaway gas increase in NMC811 cells provides a longer detection window compared to NCA cells, where the transition to thermal runaway is more abrupt.
C. NMC811 cells present a greater post-onset combustion and toxicity hazard than NCA cells, given their higher peak temperatures and substantially greater hydrocarbon release.
D. Because NMC811 cells produce more CO2 relative to CO, their gas emissions are less hazardous than NCA cells, since CO2 is non-flammable.
E. The difference in onset temperature between NCA and NMC811 is large enough to be the primary basis for selecting NMC811 as the safer chemistry for high-temperature environments.

F. NCA cells' abrupt gas ejection pattern at thermal runaway onset implies that mechanical venting designs relying on gradual pressure build-up for actuation may be less reliable for NCA than for NMC811.

solver_view.stem: Two lithium iron phosphate battery formats—one large-format (LFP-L) and one small-format (LFP-SS)—are tested under thermal runaway conditions in an inert atmosphere at 100% and 110% SOC. LFP-SS consistently produces more gas per unit capacity than LFP-L across all conditions, yet LFP-L reaches substantially higher peak temperatures. At 110% SOC, the peak temperature ratio between LFP-L and LFP-SS widens compared to 100% SOC. Which of the following conclusions are admissible given these conditions?
options: A. Higher gas production per unit capacity in LFP-SS does not necessarily correspond to higher peak temperature, indicating that gas yield and thermal severity are not directly coupled through a single shared mechanism.
B. The widening temperature ratio at 110% SOC compared to 100% SOC implies that LFP-L's thermal response is more sensitive to SOC elevation than LFP-SS.
C. Because LFP-SS generates more gas per Ah, it must release more total chemical energy during thermal runaway, making it inherently more thermally hazardous than LFP-L at equivalent capacity.
D. The consistent gas production advantage of LFP-SS across all three test conditions is sufficient to conclude that format geometry is the sole controlling variable for gas yield.
E. The observed temperature and gas yield trends together confirm that peak temperature is primarily determined by gas volume rather than by energy stored in the cell.
F. A system designer cannot assume that the format with lower gas production per Ah will also exhibit lower peak temperature, as the evidence shows the opposite relationship.
